commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: - [P1] Human PR review is needed because the branch has a P1 code defect and the external contributor still needs to provide redacted after-fix Slack proof.; Needs attention: The diff has no supply-chain change, but it introduces an authorization-boundary concern by trusting the provider id as a Slack channel name.; Review finding: [P1] Use a real Slack channel name for currentChannelName — extensions/slack/src/thre... (sha=ee834023a27bd88d5ce71b5c3c271c90b2038804)
commands.review_summary:
The PR updates Slack read-target authorization to accept channel names from context or `conversations.info`, adds Slack tests, and introduces a shared threading context name field.

PR surface: Source +99, Tests +128. Total +227 across 6 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level. Current main checks Slack read targets by channel ID only, while PR head wires the context shortcut from a field core fills with the provider id, so the intended name-allowlist path does not match the real core-built context.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Human PR review is needed because the branch has a P1 code defect and the external contributor still needs to provide redacted after-fix Slack proof.

target.title: fix(slack): allow channel-id reads for name-allowlisted channels
target.body: ## What Problem This Solves Slack action tools can target a channel by Slack channel ID, for example when reading a permalink or operating on a message outside the current thread. If an operator configured Slack channel access by human-readable channel name (for example `#allowed-channel`) with `dangerouslyAllowNameMatching` enabled, those channel-ID reads were rejected before the name allowlist could be applied. That blocked legitimate read-like Slack actions such as reading messages, downloading files, listing pins, and listing reactions for channels that were configured as allowed by name. ## How This Changes Behavior - Carries the current Slack channel name through threading tool context when the inbound transport provides it. - Uses the current channel name only when the target channel matches the current context, so one allowed context cannot authorize a different target channel. - Lazily resolves Slack conversation names through `conversations.info` when an action has only a channel ID and the account explicitly enabled name matching. - Re-checks read authorization with the resolved channel name and still fails closed if no allowed name is found. - Reuses the existing conversation info cache and returns names from that helper. ## Evidence - `pnpm test:extension slack` — 101 files / 1334 tests passed. - `pnpm vitest run extensions/slack/src/action-runtime.test.ts extensions/slack/src/channel-type.test.ts` — 2 files / 74 tests passed. - `pnpm tsgo:extensions` — passed. - `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD` — passed. - Added synthetic-fixture coverage for: - current-context channel-name authorization of a matching channel ID; - rejecting a different target channel even when the current context has an allowed name; - resolving a channel name via `conversations.info` before failing an allowlist read; - resolving a name-keyed allow before applying wildcard fallback denial; - returning conversation names from the Slack channel-type helper. Not sure if this is acceptable proof, but in the past month, I had multiple OpenClaws on different machines collaborate with one another via a shared whitelisted Slack channel. Recently (as in the past ~3-5 days), after I upgraded to 2026.6.8, the ability for me to have bot-to-bot conversations went away. My use case was Bot A does dev work on a repo; Bot B does deployment of a said PR to servers C it has access to. A does not have access to C, but can do so indirectly through B. Example of it working from the past month: Example of it NOT working: Example of it working again after this patch: I'm having a bit of difficulty patching the source, reinstalling OpenClaw from source, and then generating this proof... As it's 6am now and I haven't slept, I'll have to try to resume this later. ## Privacy / Fixture Notes All examples in this PR use fabricated Slack fixtures such as `#allowed-channel`, `C0123456789`, and `C9876543210`; no private workspace channel names, channel IDs, user IDs, or message timestamps are included.
target.author: jontsai

commands.reason: PR is not opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
